planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or root basal cuttings in early summer; Salvia x sylvestris is a sterile hybrid and must be propagated vegetatively. After the first main bloom flush, cut the entire plant back by half to two-thirds to promote a reliable second flush of bloom in late summer. It is drought-tolerant once established and performs best in lean, well-drained soils.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; woodland sage hybrid is the parent species of many of the finest cultivated garden salvias — a complex hybrid (S. nemorosa x S. pratensis) producing dense, bushy, upright plants clothed in aromatic gray-green leaves and, from late spring through summer (with reblooming after cutting back), dense upright spikes of rich violet-blue, blue-violet, or pink tubular flowers with contrasting bracts that maintain their color and decorative quality even after the flower petals drop. The named cultivars derived from this hybrid group are among the most widely planted perennials in temperate horticulture: 'May Night' ('Mainacht', deep violet-blue with dark bracts, Gold Medal Award winner), 'Blue Hill' ('Blauhugel', clear sky-blue), 'Rose Queen' (rose-pink), and 'Caradonna' (violet flowers on distinctive dark, near-black stems). Drought-tolerant, long-blooming with proper deadheading, and reliable across a wide zone range from 4 to 9 — qualities that make this hybrid group among the most useful and versatile of all garden perennials for the sunny, low-maintenance border.

planting.note: Sow seeds in early spring or divide established clumps in spring; Japanese aster is more mildew-resistant and longer-blooming than most Symphyotrichum asters and does not require staking. Divide every 3–4 years to maintain vigor. The double-flowered cultivar 'Blue Star' (white with a hint of lavender, pompon-like center) is particularly ornamental and widely available.

directions: Plant in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Japanese aster is an East Asian native that offers several practical advantages over the North American native asters (Symphyotrichum) commonly grown in temperate gardens — notably its exceptional resistance to powdery mildew (which disfigures many native asters by midsummer), its compact, non-floppy habit that rarely needs staking, and its exceptionally long bloom season from June through October, a span of nearly five months. The flowers are typical small asters — white to pale lavender-blue with a yellow central disc — but produced with remarkable freedom and duration from midsummer well into fall. The cultivar 'Blue Star' has semi-double to double flowers with a distinctive pale lavender-blue, pompon-like, quilted center surrounded by short ray petals — a unique and refined flower form unlike conventional aster flowers. Outstanding for the middle of the summer border where a reliable, long-blooming, mildew-free plant in white-to-pale-blue is needed from midsummer through fall. Often performs better as a garden plant than the more charismatic native asters due to its mildew resistance and more compact habit.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in early spring in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; sky blue aster is drought-tolerant once established and performs best in poor, dry soils that simulate its native prairie habitat. In rich, moist soil it becomes tall and floppy. Division every 3–4 years maintains vigor.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; sky blue aster is a drought-tolerant native aster of the central prairies and dry, rocky, open woods of the central and eastern United States, producing masses of small, clear sky-blue to pale-azure daisy flowers with yellow central discs from August through October — one of the truest and most saturated blues among the native fall-blooming asters. The basal leaves are large and coarse-textured but the stem leaves are smaller and clasping, and the overall plant form is more open and prairie-like than the garden asters bred for showiness. Growing in lean, dry, rocky soils in its native habitat, it is more reliably self-supporting and compact in poor, dry conditions than in the rich soils of typical garden borders. Outstanding for prairie restorations, dry meadow plantings, and late-season native plant gardens where its clear sky-blue color provides distinctive late-season color as well as essential fall nectar for migrating pollinators including monarch butterflies. Combined with late goldenrods, ironweeds, and ornamental grasses in naturalistic fall plantings.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in spring in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade; Drummond's aster is one of the few native asters that succeeds in part shade and is native to open woodlands and woodland edges of the south-central United States, making it well-adapted to zone 9a gardens in Texas and the Gulf Coast. Divide every 3–4 years to maintain vigor.

directions: Plant in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full sun; Drummond's aster is a south-central United States native — native to the open woodlands, cedar breaks, and woodland edges of Texas, Oklahoma, Arkansas, and Missouri — producing branching, hairy stems and, in September through November, masses of small, pale-lavender to white daisy flowers with yellow central discs that turn burgundy-red as they age. It is one of the most important late-season native asters for the Gulf Coast region, blooming at a time when most other perennials have finished, and native to the exact woodland conditions found in zone 9a central Texas gardens. The heart-shaped, rough-hairy basal leaves (responsible for the 'heart-leaf aster' name) are large and distinctive. Unlike many asters that require lean, dry, full-sun conditions, Drummond's aster tolerates semi-shaded, moderately moist situations in the partial shade of open woodlands and is therefore useful under deciduous trees. An excellent native fall-blooming perennial for the Gulf Coast and south-central United States, providing essential late-season nectar at the end of the monarch butterfly migration period.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in cool weather 4–6 weeks before last frost or in fall in mild climates; pink hawk's beard is a cool-season annual that performs best before summer heat sets in. It self-sows freely and can maintain naturalized colonies in mild climates by fall and spring self-sowing. It does not transplant well — always direct sow.

directions: Direct sow in cool weather in full sun in any well-drained, moderately fertile to lean soil; pink hawk's beard is a charming Italian native annual producing basal rosettes of dandelion-like toothed leaves and from spring through early summer, slender stems bearing several cheerful, clear pink to rose-pink flowers closely resembling small, elegant, pink dandelion flowers — each one individually attractive, with strap-like ray florets in clear rose-pink radiating from a central disc, all held on slender, branching stems above the foliage. In the aggregate, mass plantings create a low carpet of bright pink that is effective at close range and has the unaffected, wildflower charm of a meadow plant rather than a cultivated ornamental. It is one of the few pink-flowered members of the dandelion tribe and is grown as a cool-season annual for spring color. Self-sows freely in favorable conditions to maintain colonies. Easy to grow and useful for cottage garden edges, naturalizing in light grass, and filling gaps in spring borders before summer annuals are planted.
